moves.note: 2x space spec, tiny book, cut first
Chips flagged weak, reduce sector risk
Slight trim, free cash for buffer

plan: Sell SOXL and SPAL immediately — both leveraged ETFs are down double-digits and daily decay compounds losses in sideways markets.
Trim SMH from 13% to 7% to bring total semiconductor exposure down from 19% to roughly 10%, well within the 45% sector cap.
Trim CRWD from 12% to 7% to honor the single-name 20% ceiling and lock in some of that +7.4% gain.
Rotate freed capital into QQQI (up to 24%) for steady income and SPY (up to 32%) for broad, lower-volatility exposure.
Remaining freed cash sits as a 15%+ buffer — do not redeploy until a clear confirmed setup appears on the scanner.
conservative.read: This posture targets modest single-digit monthly participation with a meaningfully lower drawdown profile — the bad-month swing shrinks because leveraged ETFs and concentrated chip exposure are gone. The honest trade-off is that a strong tech rally will leave this book behind; income and broad index rarely match a hot sector move. Capital is protected, not compounded aggressively.

moves.note: Keep tiny leverage slug, strict stop
Space spec too small to matter, exit

plan: Sell SPAL outright — at 2% it barely moves the needle and the -13.72% print says momentum is broken.
Trim SOXL to 3% max, keeping a small leveraged chip position but with a strict stop near current scanner levels so downside is capped.
Trim SMH to 9% and CRWD to 9% so no single sector exceeds roughly 25-30% and no single name dominates.
Add to QQQI up to 20% as the income anchor; this partially offsets the vol from the remaining leveraged and tech positions.
Place conditional Watch orders on PANW (5%) and TSM (4%) — only enter if scanner entry levels trigger; combined adds do not exceed the capital freed from trims.
balanced.read: Balanced mode aims for meaningful participation in tech upside while keeping the bad-month scenario materially less painful than the current -17.91% figure. Adding quality names like PANW only on confirmed entry keeps discipline intact. This is not a guarantee of 2.5%/month — a chip sector reversal or broad market drawdown will still hurt, just less than the current concentrated book.

moves.note: Lean into chip leverage with strict…

plan: Sell SPAL — even in aggressive mode, a broken 2x ETF with no scanner setup is dead weight; redeploy that capital where the edge is clearer.

Add SOXL to 9% and SMH to 15%, leaning into the semiconductor thesis with the understanding that SOXL loses value daily through decay even if the sector goes sideways — strict stops are non-negotiable.
Add CRWD to 14%, the strongest recent performer in the book, aligned with the AI/software scanner theme.
Place Watch entries on ARM (R=9.46, top scanner score) and PANW (R=6.01, ACCUMULATE) at scanner entry levels only — do not chase above those levels.
Trim QQQ slightly to 12% to fund adds without blowing past current margin levels; total leverage stays monitored and stops on all leveraged positions are set before the trade is placed.
aggressive.read: Aggressive mode explicitly accepts that a bad month could exceed the current -17.91% estimate — adding to SOXL and concentrating further in chips means a sector leg down hits harder and faster. SOXL decays daily; it is not a buy-and-hold instrument and requires active stop management. The upside in a chip/AI rally is real, but so is the risk of losing a significant portion of equity quickly if stops are not honored.

moves.note: Keep tiny growth stub, trim most
Keep broad base, trim to fund income
Reduce growth, redirect to income ETFs

plan: Sell SOXL, SPAL, and SMH entirely — all three are high-beta, distribution-free positions that work against a low-drawdown income objective.
Trim CRWD to 4% to keep a small growth stub but free the majority of capital for income rotation.
Build QQQI to 35% as the core holding — it is already in the book, familiar, and provides monthly covered-call distributions from the Nasdaq-100 universe.
Trim QQQ to 10% and SPY to 25%; broad index stays as a NAV stabilizer and mild growth participation but no longer dominates the book.
Remaining freed capital (~16%) sits as a cash buffer — distributions are variable and NAV can fall in a selloff, so the buffer absorbs shocks without forcing you to sell income positions at a loss.
income.read: Income mode targets reliable monthly distributions rather than price appreciation — QQQI's covered-call structure generates income but caps upside in strong rallies, and NAV can still fall meaningfully in a broad selloff. A 1%/month distribution target is plausible in normal markets but is never guaranteed; covered-call ETF payouts vary with volatility and market conditions. This posture trades growth potential for stability and cash flow.
